title: Conjecture 1 (Dvořák [5])
statement_text: For every polynomial $p$, there exists a function $q : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that the following holds. Let $G$ be a graph with expansion bounded by $p$ and let $\rho : V(G) \to \mathbb{R}^+_0$ be a cost assignment for $G$. For every integer $t \geq 1$, $G$ has a balanced separator which is $(\rho/t)$-cheap with $q(t)$ outliers.
context_text: The conjecture concerns the weighted version of sublinear separators: even though a graph with polynomial expansion always has a small balanced separator, the separator may be costly under an arbitrary cost assignment $\rho$. The conjecture asks that a $(\rho/t)$-cheap balanced separator always exists after removing only a bounded number $q(t)$ of outlier vertices, with the bound depending only on $t$ and not on the number of vertices. It is noted to be a weakening of the author's earlier conjecture that graphs with polynomial expansion are fractionally treewidth-fragile.
paper_contribution: Proves that for every polynomial $p$ and integer $a \geq 0$ there exists a polynomial $q$ such that $G$ has a balanced separator which is $(\rho/t)$-cheap with $\exp(q(t))\log^{(a)} n$ outliers (Theorem 3), and with $q(t)\,\mathrm{wcol}_{5q(t)}(G)\log^{(a)} n$ outliers in terms of weak coloring numbers (Theorem 4), improving the prior bound of $q(t \log n)$ outliers and replacing the $\log n$ dependence by any fixed iterated logarithm.
notes: Conjecture originally stated by the same author in prior work [5]. PDF source — math reconstructed from context; notation appears clean.
